raw_output_payload.reasoning:
We need to produce a bash command that does this. Input string: "1/2 [3] (27/03/2012 19:32:54) word word word word 4/5". Remove everything within parentheses. So remove "(27/03/2012 19:32:54)". Then substitute all non-digit characters with a space. So digits remain. After removal, string becomes "1/2 [3]  word word word word 4/5". Then replace non-digits with space: digits and spaces? Non-digit replaced by space. So digits remain, others become space. So "1/2 [3]  word word word word 4/5" -> digits: 1 2 3 4 5. But slashes and brackets become spaces. So result: "1 2   3     4 5". Then format as a table. Maybe use printf? Table maybe columns? Probably just output as table with numbers separated by spaces? Maybe use awk to print each number in column. Let's produce: echo "1/2 [3] (27/03/2012 19:32:54) word word word word 4/5" | sed 's/([^)]*)//g' | tr -cd '0-9
' | tr '
' ' ' | awk '{print $1,$2,$3,$4,$5}'? But need table. Maybe use column -t. So final command: echo ... | sed 's/([^)]*)//g' | tr -cd '0-9
' | tr '
' ' ' | column -t. But tr -cd removes non-digits, but we want spaces for non-digits. Instead use tr -s '[:digit:]' ' '?? Actually we want replace non-digit with space: tr -c '0-9' ' '. So: echo ... | sed 's/([^)]*)//g' | tr -c '0-9' ' ' | column -t. That will produce table with numbers. Let's output.
